summary: Gruber celebrates the Mac's 30th anniversary by examining two quintessentially Apple qualities visible in the original Macintosh: obsessive attention to detail and carefully calibrated whimsy. He traces how the keyboard shortcuts Z, X, C, V were chosen not just mnemonically but ergonomically, with their Edit menu order matching their physical keyboard position — a design that persists unchanged today. He then explores the Finder's 'Special' menu as an example of Apple's taste in naming, arguing the word served both as a gentle warning and as a description of the Mac itself. A footnote offers a sharp critique of iOS 7 as having over-corrected away from whimsy.
key_points: The original Mac's design decisions — from menu bar structure to keyboard shortcuts — persist largely unchanged 30 years later, testifying to their brilliance
The Z/X/C/V shortcuts were assigned by physical keyboard position and ergonomic convenience rather than mnemonic meaning, prioritizing how frequently they'd be used in tandem
The Finder's 'Special' menu exemplified Apple's taste: a word that served as both a gentle warning about dangerous commands and an apt description of the Mac itself
Apple's signature whimsy — from the smiling Mac to iOS app jiggle — requires taste to land as friendly rather than goofy
iOS 7's mixed reception stemmed less from specific design critiques than from a general depletion of whimsy, over-correcting from iOS 6's skeuomorphic excess
money_quotes: The greatest testimony to their genius is just how much of that original design is recognizable in today's Mac OS X 10.9.
You remember these shortcuts not by letter, but by physical position.
Even these four commands' order in the Edit menu corresponded to their shortcuts' order on the keyboard: Undo, Cut, Copy, Paste — Z, X, C, V. Simply brilliant.
Fundamental to the genius of the smiling Mac is that it didn't come across as silly or corny. Friendly and fun: yes. Goofy: no. Getting that right required that most Apple-y of talents: taste.
More than serving as an apt categorization of the menu items it contained, "Special" served a higher purpose — an apt description of the Macintosh in its entirety.
When I see those four words, they evoke not a thought but a feeling. Poetry, in the form of a menu bar.
It's the depletion of whimsy in general that sits at the heart of the mixed reaction that greeted iOS 7.
key_insight: The original Macintosh's enduring genius lies in its obsessive attention to ergonomic detail and its carefully calibrated whimsy — qualities that define Apple at its best and whose absence is felt when Apple loses its way.
